metadata.description: General non-negotiation rapport_building pairs for probing only. Tests whether the rapport_building vector generalises beyond negotiation contexts to everyday social interactions.
metadata.note: Contexts deliberately avoid negotiation, pricing, contracts, and business deals.

dimensions.name: Rapport Building
dimensions.description: Establishing trust, connection, and warmth; finding common ground; making people feel genuinely seen

pairs.context: You are introduced to a new colleague on their first day at work.
pairs.positive: Welcome! I remember my first week here being a bit overwhelming. If you ever have questions or just want to know the unwritten rules, feel free to drop by.
pairs.negative: Hi. If you need anything related to IT or facilities, the contact details are in the welcome pack.

pairs.context: You are at a social event where you don't know many people and a person is standing alone.
pairs.positive: Hi — I don't think we've met. I'm trying to find the people who are also quietly wondering when it's acceptable to leave. Are you in that camp too?
pairs.negative: Hi, I'm David. Are you here with someone or did you come alone?

pairs.context: A patient arrives at a clinic looking anxious and sits down in the waiting area.
pairs.positive: Hi, I can see you're waiting for your appointment. It's perfectly normal to feel nervous in here. Is there anything I can get you while you wait?
pairs.negative: Your appointment is at 2:30. You'll be called when the doctor is ready.

pairs.context: You are meeting a potential mentor for the first time over coffee.
pairs.positive: I really appreciate you making time for this. I've been following your work for a while and what drew me to reaching out was something specific you said about the transition between early and mid-career. It resonated.
pairs.negative: Thanks for meeting me. I have a few questions about your career path that I wanted to ask you.

pairs.context: A student in your class looks isolated and doesn't join group discussions.
pairs.positive: I noticed you had some really interesting notes last session — I'd genuinely like to hear your take on this one if you're comfortable sharing.
pairs.negative: The exercise is a group discussion. Please make sure everyone contributes.

pairs.context: You are speaking with an elderly patient who seems lonely and hasn't had a visitor in a while.
pairs.positive: How are you really doing? And I mean beyond the medical stuff — how's the day to day? It sounds like you've been here a while.
pairs.negative: I'm here to check your vitals and review the medication. How are you feeling physically?

pairs.context: A new volunteer joins the team and seems uncertain about where they fit in.
pairs.positive: I'm glad you're here. We were the same size team for two years and it makes a real difference to have someone new. What made you want to get involved?
pairs.negative: Welcome. Here's a brief on what we do. Let me know if you have any questions once you've had a chance to read it.

pairs.context: You run into an old acquaintance you haven't seen in several years.
pairs.positive: It's so good to see you — it's been years. The last time I saw you you were about to move abroad. How did that go? I've thought about that conversation since.
pairs.negative: Oh hey — long time. Are you still in the same line of work?

pairs.context: A job applicant arrives for an interview looking visibly nervous.
pairs.positive: Come on in. Interviews are genuinely odd situations and most people find them stressful. Take a moment to settle and we'll take this at a reasonable pace.
pairs.negative: Please take a seat. We'll start with a brief overview of the role and then move to the questions.

pairs.context: You are chairing a first meeting of a new project team who don't all know each other.
pairs.positive: Before we get into the work, let's take five minutes to say something real about ourselves — not just our job titles. I want us to know who we're actually working with.
pairs.negative: Let's go around the table with names and roles so everyone knows who's who, and then we'll get into the project brief.

pairs.context: A student visits during office hours looking stressed about more than just the assignment.
pairs.positive: Something tells me this isn't just about the essay. How are you actually doing at the moment? There's no rush — we have time.
pairs.negative: What specifically are you struggling with on the assignment? Let's try to focus the session so you get the most out of it.

pairs.context: You are at a networking event and someone approaches you looking uncertain about who to talk to.
pairs.positive: These events are a bit much, aren't they? I've been standing here deciding whether the drinks are free enough to justify this level of forced conversation. What brings you here?
pairs.negative: Hi, I'm Sarah. What sector are you in?

pairs.context: A team member returns from sick leave after a serious illness.
pairs.positive: It's really good to have you back. We've missed you — and I mean as a person, not just for the workload. How are you actually feeling?
pairs.negative: Good to have you back. We'll do a catch-up on what you've missed and figure out how to get you back up to speed.

pairs.context: You are running a community workshop and one participant seems disengaged from the start.
pairs.positive: I want to check in with you — I noticed you've been pretty quiet. Is the session landing okay for you, or is there something about the format that's not working?
pairs.negative: Let's make sure everyone is participating. The exercises work better when we have full engagement from the group.

pairs.context: You are having coffee with a colleague before a difficult team meeting.
pairs.positive: I wanted to grab you before we go in. Whatever happens in there, I want you to know I've got your back and I think we've got a good thing going on this team.
pairs.negative: I think the key points we need to make are X and Y. Shall we align on those before we go in?
